summary:
The West African Neolithic refers to the period of Neolithic cultural development in the region of present-day West Africa (encompassing the Sahel, savanna, and forest zones) between approximately 5000 and 2000 BCE. During this period, communities across the region developed or adopted agriculture, ceramic production, polished stone tool technology, and sedentary or semi-sedentary lifestyles adapted to the varied environments of the region from the southern Sahara to the tropical forest margin.

West Africa was an independent center of plant domestication. African species including pearl millet, sorghum, African rice, fonio, black-eyed peas, and several other crops were domesticated in the Sahel and savanna zones of West Africa, largely independently of Near Eastern agricultural origins. This indigenous agricultural tradition formed the economic foundation for later West African civilizations including the Ghana, Mali, and Songhai empires. The development of West African Neolithic cultures thus represents an autonomous contribution to the global story of the agricultural revolution.

The West African Neolithic is less thoroughly documented archaeologically than Neolithic cultures of the Near East or Europe, partly because acidic tropical soils preserve organic materials poorly and partly because intensive archaeological research in the region has had fewer resources. Nonetheless, sites such as those associated with the Kintampo complex in Ghana provide clear evidence of established Neolithic communities with domestic animals, cultivated plants, and ceramic production by at least 3500 BCE. West Africa's Neolithic communities laid the cultural and demographic foundations for one of the world's most diverse and complex cultural landscapes.
